response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm not on social media much, maybe a few times a month when I have a reason to check in, and honestly that's probably why I don't get too worked up about fake content. When you're only popping in occasionally, you're not getting sucked into every drama or conspiracy theory that's making the rounds. I work as a mechanic, and in my line of work, things either function correctly or they don't. There's no middle ground when a brake system fails or an engine misfires. I approach social media the same way, with a healthy dose of skepticism about what I'm seeing. If something sounds too dramatic or too perfect, I just scroll past it. I don't have time to fact-check every post, and frankly, I don't think most people do either. The way I see it, if you're spending hours every day on these platforms, you're setting yourself up to be manipulated. My approach is simple and it works for me. I stick to following a handful of accounts that are actually useful, like local shops or photography pages related to my hobby. I don't follow random people or pages that post sensational garbage. When I do see something that seems off, I just ignore it rather than engaging or sharing it. Getting into arguments with strangers online about what's real or fake seems like a waste of energy. I've also learned that if something important is actually happening, you'll hear about it through multiple channels, not just one sketchy post. My husband and I talk about news stories when they come up, and that real conversation is worth more than a hundred social media posts. The bottom line is that people need to take responsibility for what they consume and share. Social media companies could do more, sure, but at the end of the day, if you're gullible enough to believe everything you read online without questioning it, that's on you. Stay skeptical, don't spend all your time scrolling, and use common sense. That's my take on it.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 33-year-old female mechanic with low openness, low extraversion, low agreeableness, and moderate conscientiousness. She spends minimal time on social media (few times monthly), has no formal education, and is an atheist with good mental health. Her practical, hands-on occupation as a mechanic and limited social media engagement suggest a straightforward, no-nonsense approach. Low openness indicates preference for traditional, proven methods and skepticism toward new trends. Low agreeableness suggests directness and willingness to express unpopular opinions without sugar-coating. Low extraversion means she's not seeking social validation online. Her hobbies (knitting, photography) are solitary activities. The tone should be pragmatic, somewhat blunt, self-reliant, and skeptical. She would likely emphasize personal responsibility over systemic solutions, given her low agreeableness. Her mechanical work provides a useful metaphor for her black-and-white thinking. Limited social media use is a key advantage in her view. She wouldn't be preachy but would be direct about her perspective.
response_parsed.tone: pragmatic, direct, slightly blunt, skeptical, self-reliant
